A one-dollar copayment cuts clinic visits by nearly 10 percent. The question is who, exactly, stops going.  ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌

June 09, 2026   |   Read online

How Would Healthcare Change If Everyone Had To Pay A $1 Copayment?

A one-dollar copayment cuts clinic visits by nearly 10 percent. The question is who, exactly, stops going.

Hey! You’re receiving this newsletter as one of 7,961 subscribers (+309 thank you to everyone who singed up.)

We have one of our bigger videos ever coming at the end of the month. I am excited to share it with you. Be on the look out!

You’ll find the “Share the Newsletter” feature at the end of the current issue. Today’s newsletter contains 729 words, and reading it in full takes about 4 and a half minutes.

 

Economists have a word for what happens when you don't pay for something yourself: moral hazard.

The idea is that when someone else covers the bill, you use more than you would otherwise.  

So you book the extra appointment and fill the prescription you weren't sure you needed — because why wouldn't you? Free healthcare, the theory goes, encourages overuse, and overuse is expensive.

The solution, in theory, is obvious. Charge people something. Anything. Just enough to make the decision feel like a decision.

In the 1970s, the United States ran what remains the largest randomised experiment in the history of healthcare economics. Around 6,000 families were assigned to insurance plans ranging from completely free to requiring patients to cover nearly all costs out of pocket.

Sure enough, people on the free plan used roughly 30 percent more healthcare than those with the highest cost-sharing.

That principle has since been confirmed at much smaller scales. Introduce a copayment of exactly one dollar into a public health programme for low-income individuals, and outpatient clinic visits fall by 9.8%. Introduce a fifty-cent charge at the pharmacy, and you cut visits by 8.1%.

So the theory holds: charge people something and they use it less.

But the story gets more complicated.

The care that disappears after introducing a copayment as small as $1 is not random. Unnecessary antibiotic prescriptions fall. Same with clinic visits for minor complaints that would have been resolved on their own. But high-value care, like medication adherence for chronic illnesses or follow-ups for serious conditions, barely moves.

It's a hopeful result, if you stop there. A small copayment reduces wasteful care without touching necessary treatment.

That's exactly what good policy is supposed to do. Except that only works if a dollar is nothing to you.

A significant share of low-income individuals don't seek healthcare even when it is completely free, and not because they're healthy. The actual cost of getting care, once you factor in transport and a shift lost at work, is already high enough to make the whole exercise feel not worth the effort.

So when you add a dollar to that equation, you are changing the behaviour of someone who was barely using it at all. The person who drops off is not a middle-income professional making a smarter calculation. More often it’s the person for whom a dollar — and the accumulated friction around it — tips the balance from "I should probably go" to "I'll wait and see."

Now, the United States is in the middle of a significant restructuring of Medicaid. Work requirements being phased in could remove coverage for around five million people, and proposed changes would see some low-income enrollees paying $35 per service within a few years.

And we already know what happens when you run that experiment.

In 2003, Oregon added copayments, trimmed benefits, and raised premiums for its Medicaid population. Within months, about half of those enrollees had left the programme, most ended up uninsured, and emergency room use went up.

A small copayment is not inherently destructive policy. The evidence suggests it can reduce low-value care without blocking essential treatment, but only when the charge is small enough that it functions as a signal rather than a barrier. And thirty-five dollars, to a household at the poverty line, is not small.

 

Here's Why We're Not Hyping the SpaceX IPO

Most newsletters are selling you a SpaceX story. We're walking through the numbers.

Our free analyst briefing breaks down three valuation scenarios, comparable late-stage listings (ARM, Reddit, Klaviyo), and the price ranges where retail entry actually makes sense.

Read the Sober Analysis

 

How AI Will Play Out, Explained

In 2019, we made a video asking what a fully automated world would actually look like. Since then, things have changed a lot.

When AI started, the threat felt most acute for the repetitive, manual work that machines had always been most eager to take on.

But what happened instead was almost the opposite. By the time we revisited it last year, AI had become a threat for the outsourced service economies that developing countries had spent three decades carefully building.

Now, in our latest video, we revisit seven years of predictions — what we got right, what we missed, and what an MIT study published earlier this year changed about how we understand all of it.
